pair.title: Tell HN: I'm sick of AI everything

story.title: Tell HN: I'm sick of AI everything

comments.text:
I actually think there's a meaningful difference here. During dotcom, the hype was mostly detached from reality-Pets.com was never going to work at that scale with those economics. But AI tooling is genuinely useful for a lot of engineering work right now. The problem isn't that the tech is fake; it's that every company is using it as a brand band-aid instead of actually thinking about what it solves for them.

The job posting thing especially bugs me because it signals the opposite of what they probably intend. If your value prop to engineers is "we use AI," that tells me you haven't figured out what your actual problems are yet. The companies I'm genuinely interested in working for mention AI only when it's solving a specific bottleneck they've identified. Usually in a one-liner, not a whole section.

I agree with you that fixing organizational dysfunction first would help immensely. Most places I've seen trying to "AI-enable" their processes are just autom

I've been hiring engineers for the past 5 years and I can confirm this is real. What's interesting is that the AI language in job postings correlates almost perfectly with how immature their actual ML practice is. The teams that are genuinely doing sophisticated ML work tend to describe it in technical specifics: "we're optimizing our ranking model with learned-to-rank techniques" or "we maintain a feature store with 40k+ features for our recommendation pipeline." The ones just spraying "AI-powered" everywhere usually haven't shipped anything beyond a RAG wrapper or some GPT API calls. It's almost a screening signal at this point. When I see a posting that's half AI buzzwords

kids.text:
This is exactly the pattern I'm seeing. The teams actually shipping meaningful ML have the technical vocabulary to show for it. They don't need to say "AI-powered" because their job spec reads like an engineering doc.

The frustrating part is it makes hiring harder for everyone-real signal gets drowned out

comments.text: The real tell is how job postings went from "we're building X for customers who need Y" to "we use AI" as if that's a value prop. Nobody cares that your stack includes an LLM. They care if your product actually works better because of it. The dotcom parallel is

kids.text: The dotcom parallel breaks down here though. Pets.com was wrong about logistics, not about whether people wanted pet supplies. But a lot of these "AI-powered" job postings are claiming AI solves problems that don't actually exist yet, or that existed fine before. That's closer to Koz

Kozmo actually had a harder problem to solve operationally (same-day delivery in 1999), but you're right that the core demand was real. The AI case is murkier because we're conflating three things: (1) genuine algorithmic improvements that happened to use neural nets, (2) chatbot wrappers around existing CRUD apps, and (3) pure marketing theater.

Job postings mostly live in bucket 2 or 3. A hiring manager who says "we're an AI-first company" without specifying what inference loop actually moves the needle is basically admitting they don't know why they're using it. That's the real warning sign. The dotcom founders at least *believed* in their vert

The job posting thing is a symptom of deeper cargo cult thinking. Companies see that AI is getting attention and assume the attention itself is the product. So they jam it into every marketing surface hoping it sticks.

But you're right that dotcom had an excuse: the underlying infrastructure (broadband, payment processing, logistics) genuinely didn't exist yet. Companies were building toward a real future, just most of them burned out before getting there.

With AI, we're mostly watching people bolt ChatGPT onto existing workflows and call it innovation. The organizational problems stay put. If your customer support is terrible because you don't pay people enough to care, an LLM won't fix that, it'll just make the support terrible faster.

What's different now is the hype cycle is compressed and

comments.text: The real issue here isn't hype, it's regulatory capture. Companies aren't mandated to disclose AI usage in hiring, training data sourcing, or decision-making. The dotcom bubble at least had the SEC watching. We need EU-style transparency rules before this gets worse. What gets measured gets

kids.text:
Good point on transparency. I think you're right that disclosure requirements would help, though I'm skeptical they'd move the needle on the actual hype cycle. Companies will just get creative with language.

What I'm more worried about is the second-order effect: if every job posting says "AI-driven this" and "machine learning that," how do candidates even parse signal from noise? The dotcom bubble at least had a technological excuse-the internet was genuinely new. This feels more like collective delusion about what's actually novel versus repackaged.

The regulatory angle is worth pushing on though. You're right the SEC wasn't watching closely enough in '99. If we're going to have serious AI infrastructure companies, they should have to disclose actual usage, benchmarks, and failure rates in their hiring/evaluation tools, not just marketing language. That would probably kill 60% of the "AI-enabled" claims overnight.

Regulatory capture lol. The SEC has been neutered for 20 years and the EU can barely enforce GDPR. Companies will do whatever maximizes quarterly earnings and settle fines later. The dotcom had actual product demand underneath-people genuinely wanted to buy stuff online. This is just marketing theater

I worked at a company during the 2008 crisis that had pivoted hard into "green tech" right before everything collapsed. We spent months rewriting every piece of marketing copy to emphasize sustainability, hired a whole team around it, then laid most of them off within a year when the funding dried up. This feels exactly like that-the panic move to stay relevant

comments.text:
I was a junior dev during the late 90s at a mid-size e-commerce startup. We had investors telling us to slap "Internet" or ".com" into every pitch deck, regardless of whether it made sense. The CEO once spent an entire board meeting arguing that our logistics problem would solve itself once we "got big enough." It didn't.

What strikes me about the current AI cycle is the speed and depth of the cargo cult behavior. Back then, at least the underlying technology-the web itself-was genuinely transformative, even if most companies had no idea what to do with it. Now I'm seeing job postings that say things like "AI-powered customer support" when they mean a chatbot that's just a worse version of their FAQ, or "AI-driven analytics" when they mean they're using an LLM to write SQL queries (badly).

The organizational dysfunction angle you mentioned is spot on. I've watched companies implement these tools without fixing the actual problems. You add Claude to a meeting where nobody actually talks to each other, and now you've just got well-written silence. One client hired an LLM to "enhance their content pipeline" when their real problem was that their writers had quit because management was impossible. They burned through their budget on API calls within six months.

The job posting thing bothers me more than the hype cycle itself. It tells me the company is marketing to investors (or VCs) rather than actually thinking about what they need to hire. When I see that kind of copy, I assume they're either desperate for capital or ran out of ideas about what actually makes their product different. Both are yellow flags.

kids.text: I was at a mid-stage SaaS company in 2021-2022 when the "AI pivot" started. We had a perfectly functional product, decent margins, real customers. Then the board decided we needed to "AI-first" everything. Spent six months bolting GPT-2 into features nobody asked for. Lost two senior engineers who thought it was absurd. The

This is what happens when you have more money than sense. You bolt on whatever's trendy and hope the quarterly numbers move. I've seen this exact pattern three times now-dotcom, mobile, crypto-and every cycle someone acts shocked when the bolted-on feature doesn't fix broken fundamentals. The

comments.text: The funny part is that job postings weren't honest about what they needed before either-they just said "synergy" and "disruptive" instead of "AI-powered." Same packaging, different buzzword. At least now when a company leads with AI in their pitch, you know they've got nothing

kids.text: Fair point, though I'd argue there's a difference. "Synergy" was at least aspirational bullshit-companies knew it was hollow. With AI, I think many actually believe the hype. The job postings aren't cynical, they're confused. Which might be worse.

I worked at a startup during the 2015-2016 "machine learning everything" phase, and this absolutely mirrors that. We had a perfectly functional recommendation system built on collaborative filtering. Then the new VP of Product came in, declared we needed "deep learning," and we spent six months building a neural network that performed identically to the old system but took three times longer to train.

The difference is that back then, at least the internal pressure was contained. Now it's baked into recruiting and investor narratives, so the confusion spreads outward. Job descriptions get written by people who've internalized the hype without understanding what it actually solves. You end up with posts asking for "AI engineers" to do work that's fundamentally just data plumbing.

The cynicism might actually be preferable to the genuine belief. At least you knew where you stood.

"Synergy" and "disruptive" were at least vague enough that you could imagine them meaning something. "AI-powered" is actively misleading in most job postings because it usually just means "we bought a ChatGPT subscription and now everyone has to use it for everything."

The difference is that dotcom buzzwords were aspirational cover for real business problems (logistics, discovery, matching). AI buzzwords are cover for the fact that companies haven't actually identified a problem yet. They're buying the solution first and retrofitting the use case.

The difference is that dot-com at least had the excuse of being new. Nobody knew what the internet would do. This is just copy-paste hype from a playbook that's been run three times already (social media, blockchain, metaverse). Andreessen and crew figured out that if you attach a hot enough label to your fund's portfolio companies, LPs stop asking hard questions about unit economics.

The pet food thing is instructive though. Kozmo failed but the *actual demand* for online pet supplies was real and got solved by Amazon and Chewy. With AI, I keep waiting for that moment where it's obvious what the actual demand is. So far it's mostly "we added ChatGPT to our SaaS" and "we fine-tuned a model on your data" which... yeah, that's not a moat and it's definitely not a business. P

> Andreessen and crew figured out that if you attach a hot enough label to your fund's portfolio companies, LPs stop asking hard questions about unit economics.

But this has always been true. They said the same thing about cloud, mobile, social. The difference is those actually worked out for enough companies that the hype, in retrospect, looked prescient. AI might too. Or it might

Fair point, though I'd push back on the equivalence a bit. "Synergy" and "disruptive" were at least transparent nonsense-everyone knew what you were getting. With AI, there's actual ambiguity about what's being claimed.

A job posting that says "we use AI to optimize our routing" could mean anything from "we retrained a model last quarter" to "we replaced five engineers with Claude." The recruiter probably doesn't know either. That uncertainty is worse than dotcom buzzword inflation because it makes it harder to filter signal from noise.

The real test is whether they can articulate what problem the AI is solving. If they can't do that in the job description, they definitely haven't solved it internally.

I actually looked at this empirically last month. Checked ten "AI-optimized" job postings from Series B startups, pulled their engineering repos where public, and traced what they actually shipped.

Two had legitimate model work (one did fine-tune a Llama variant for internal tooling).

comments.text: The FTC should probably start looking at how "AI-first" positioning in job specs relates to wage suppression and skill requirement inflation. We saw similar dynamics during the dot-com era with "required experience in technologies that didn't exist two years prior." At least then the SEC eventually cracked down on some of the egregious claims. Right now there's no real enforcement mechanism for "AI-enhanced" marketing copy that's just the same product with a chatbot bolted on. Cory Doctorow

kids.text: The Cory Doctorow name-drop is doing a lot of work here but I think he's missing the actual mechanism. It's not FTC-level fraud (yet), it's just that HR discovered a magic word. "AI-enhanced" costs nothing to add to a job posting and makes

I'm not sure "HR discovered a magic word" quite captures it. The real mechanism is that job postings are now filtering tools for both sides. A candidate sees "AI-enhanced" and either thinks "this company is ahead of the curve" or "this is buzzword soup"-and the company gets

The FTC enforcement angle is interesting, but the real issue is that "AI-enhanced" isn't a false advertising claim the way "Kozmo will ship in 30 minutes" was. It's technically true-there's probably some ML somewhere in the pipeline. The dotcom comparison breaks down because those companies made falsifiable promises about delivery logistics

I think you're conflating two separate problems. The FTC angle isn't about false advertising of the feature itself, it's about whether companies are using "AI-first" positioning to justify wage suppression or to screen out candidates who could actually do the work without it. That's the dotcom parallel worth examining.

The dotcom comparison breaks down pretty fast though. Back then you at least had plausible business models that just got executed terribly or at absurd valuations. Pet food delivery makes sense; Kozmo's problem was logistics, not the idea itself.

With AI in job specs, it's often literally

I think you're framing this as a regulatory problem when it's really a signaling problem. Companies aren't trying to deceive the FTC-they're competing for talent by claiming they're "innovative" and "forward-thinking." The job specs are cargo cult stuff, yes, but the root issue is that engineers (reasonably) want to work on interesting problems, not legacy CRUD apps. So every

The dotcom comparison breaks down pretty quick though. Kozmo and Pets.com were solving real logistics problems (just ahead of their time), whereas most "AI-enhanced" job postings are just describing the same work, same skills, same output.
